findings.description: No brand_rule rows were extracted for this entire 'Accessibility (brand-mandated)' block despite it containing multiple distinct, clearly hard-mandated rules (type size, line height, contrast, forms, animation, media alt text, reference standard). This is a total clustering miss.

payload.text: Accessibility requirements: body copy ≥16px, line height ≥150%, use type scale, test contrast, form labels always visible with ≥48px tap targets, controlled animation with pause option, descriptive alt text and transcripts, WCAG as reference standard.

findings.description: The explicit governance/reference standard statement ('Reference standard: WCAG (wcag.com)') is not captured anywhere; since no rules exist, this citation-of-authority facet is entirely missing, leaving all accessibility rules without their compliance basis documented.

Unit 0006 mixes a brand-wide animation guideline (quick transitions, avoid excessive motion, pause option, responsive design) with a channel-scoped sub-rule explicitly marked '[GENERAL email]' (GIF frame-1 messaging, no autoplay >5s without static fallback). If clustered as a single rule, the email-specific scope would be incorrectly applied brand-wide (or vice versa) — these must be split into two rules with different scope facets.

rule_a.text: Animations: quick transitions that don't interrupt flow; avoid excessive motion; provide a pause mechanism; design responsively for phone/tablet/desktop.

rule_b.text: Animated GIFs must carry key message in frame 1; no autoplaying motion longer than ~5s without a static fallback.

findings.description: These units contain both a hard numeric minimum (≥16px, ≥150%) and a soft qualitative extension ('bigger is better', 'more for light-on-dark text'). A single merged rule risks losing the hard/soft polarity distinction — the minimum should be tagged hard while the extension guidance should be tagged soft/preferred.

Unit 0004 bundles three distinct actionable directives (test contrast; never rely on color alone; proof all text for legibility) that could be split into separate rules for clarity of hardness/scope, though they may reasonably remain merged as one contrast-related rule — flagged for review.
